query: definition and use of obturator in cleft palate treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Definitive management of cleft palate is achieved through surgical intervention, with the overall goal of separating the nasal cavity from the oral cavity and providing a palate with enough length to prevent velopharyngeal insufficiency and potentially restore eustachian tube dilator function to the palatal sling muscles. No single surgical technique is preferred to accomplish these goals, and an astute cleft surgeon will master as many as possible to tailor the surgical intervention to each specific patient. Precise timing can vary from country to country and cleft center to cleft center, but several common goals guide treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Lip -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of patients with cleft lip deformity is a long-term commitment. Medical treatment will largely focus on requirements from any concomitant congenital abnormalities and based on nutritional needs. Within the first few weeks to months of life, NAM can be employed with assistance from an orthodontist. This involves the creation of an orthodontic appliance that molds a protruding premaxillary segment and alveolar process into a more favorable position. This allows for repositioning of the alveolar segments, medialization of the alar base, and columellar lengthening, which allows for easier surgical repair of cleft lip and nasal cleft deformity down the line. These require frequent adjustments by the orthodontist. Other treatment adjuncts that assist with decreasing the severity/width of the cleft early on are lip taping (often performed in patients with less severe clefts) and lip adhesion (an approximation of the cleft lip edges without changing lip landmarks or disturbing tissue required for definitive closure, often used in patients with wide clefts who are poor NAM candidates for social or geographic reasons).

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Treatment / Management -- Bilateral Cleft Palate
retrieved_docs.content: By definition, a bilateral cleft palate involves the primary palate and can merge with the secondary palate in the form of a single cleft (see Image . Bilateral Cleft Palate). As a bilateral cleft means the prolabial segment is pedicled on the nasal septum and philtral portion of the lip, preservation of the blood supply to this segment is paramount when planning any surgery, as it is inherently tenuous.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperatively, for a CL/P, the lip is usually taped from about 1 week of life until surgery. This pseudo-recreation of the orbicularis oris helps narrow the cleft segments and improve overall symmetry; some centers will perform a surgical lip adhesion to achieve this function in preparation for later definitive palatoplasty. [37] NAM is an alternative and more aggressive measure to lip taping, which can lead to an improved cosmetic and functional outcome after surgery. NAM involves the placement of a prosthesis that is fitted from a maxillary impression and is worn 24 hours a day and adjusted weekly or biweekly. NAM can result in far improved nasal symmetry and alveolar alignment. However, it is a significant time and effort commitment for families, and poor compliance can significantly impact outcomes. Please see StatPearls' companion resources, " Cleft Palate Repair " and " Cleft Lip Repair, " for more detailed information on 2-stage lip repair and surgical cleft lip repair. [10] [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Complications -- Complications Due to Palatoplasty
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the size and location of the postoperative fistula, revision surgery, obturator placement, or a combination may be required. [68] Orthodontic obturator placement can also serve as a stop-gap to facilitate normal speech development in young patients while awaiting a suitable time for a major revision palatoplasty. Persistent oronasal fistulae postoperatively pose the same spectrum of complications as an unrepaired cleft, including speech, swallowing, and nutritional problems.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Lip Repair -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Before definitive surgical repair, techniques may be employed that facilitate surgical intervention, especially for wide clefts. The most widely used techniques are tape adhesion and orthopedic appliances. [5] The primary goal of presurgical techniques is the narrowing of the cleft to minimize lip tension after repair and improve outcomes. Tape adhesion consists of the application of steri strips across the cleft to achieve this goal. Orthopedic appliances, also termed nasoalveolar molding, consists of wearing a palatal appliance constructed by an orthodontist. This palatal appliance brings the alveolar arches closer together and narrows the cleft. The palatal appliance is gradually changed over weeks to months to optimize cleft width for surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: After birth, neonates with CL/P will require evaluation and care from an interprofessional healthcare team. The initial assessment is focused on respiratory and feeding issues to ensure the infant can grow and develop appropriately until the definitive surgical repair. Neonates need to be evaluated for sleep apnea, and parents counseled on appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: Pursed-lip Breathing -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: During exhalation, airway collapse in noncartilaginous areas increases airway resistance, leading to carbon dioxide trapping. [3] Elevated carbon dioxide levels stimulate the central chemoreceptors to increase the respiratory rate to restore pH balance to approximately 7.4. [4] [5] While this compensatory mechanism clears carbon dioxide, it may exacerbate air trapping and respiratory muscle fatigue. [6] By creating an artificial splint, PEEP prevents airway collapse, supports alveolar patency, increases surface area for gas exchange, and recruits additional alveoli for effective ventilation. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Treatment Planning -- Long-Term Cleft Care
retrieved_docs.content: The management of cleft palate ideally involves prenatal and postnatal treatment with an interprofessional team. The delineation of interventions and specialties involved varies according to each patient's condition and may be similar to the following timeline: [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Treatment Planning -- Long-Term Cleft Care
retrieved_docs.content: Age 1 to 4 years : Close follow-up for language development, dental evaluation

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Treatment Planning -- Long-Term Cleft Care
retrieved_docs.content: Age 4 to 6 years : Evaluation for palate revision/speech surgery, possible GPP or bone grafting of alveolar cleft, lip scar revision

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Treatment Planning -- Long-Term Cleft Care
retrieved_docs.content: Age 9 to 12 months : Palate repair at 11 to 12 months, ventilating tube placement if needed and not already done, ongoing speech therapy for feeding

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Lip -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical intervention for initial cleft lip usually occurs at 3 to 5 months of age. A good rule of thumb in deciding the age at which is it safe to perform primary cleft lip repair is the "Rule of 10s.” If the infant is ten weeks old, 10 pounds, and hemoglobin has reached 10mg/dL, surgical repair should be safe if no other comorbidities preclude it. There are many accepted surgical techniques for primary repair of unilateral (Millard repair rotation advancement, Fisher repair, and Mohler repair) and bilateral (Mulliken repair) cleft lips, and the surgical details are out of the scope of this article. However, common goals in all repairs are to re-establish a competent orbicularis oris muscle, lengthen the philtrum and lip, and minimize visible scarring. In primary cleft repair, some surgeons perform gingivoperiosteoplasty, which involved the elevation of the mucoperiosteal flaps along an alveolar segment with wide-undermining to promote bone growth along the periosteum, but this is not a universal practice. [10] [11] Because many of these children also have otitis media, it is important to have an ENT specialist involved in the care. During the cleft lip repair, ventilation tubes are placed but children continue to have eustachian tube dysfunction for many years.

retrieved_docs.title: Speech Assessment -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Speech assessment is a method to evaluate and diagnose problems in adults and children of speaking, swallowing, comprehension, and writing. Such evaluation may be warranted due to neurological diseases, stroke, trauma, injury, tumors, cerebral palsy, cleft palate, orofacial musculature, hearing impairment, stammering, articulation errors, motor speech disorders, and many other conditions. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate Repair -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: There are no absolute contraindications for cleft palate repair. Relative contraindications include any medical conditions that preclude using general anesthesia (cardiac conditions, severe illness, etc.). Some patients with airway conditions or syndromes may need to be addressed before the palatoplasty, such as patients with retrognathia, Pierre Robin sequence, etc.

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, while CL/P and CPO frequently occur in isolation, in up to 30% of cases, these defects can also be part of a congenital syndrome, which must be recognized to initiate early interprofessional management. [1] This resource primarily discusses an overview of cleft palate. Please see StatPearls' companion resources, " Cleft Palate Repair ," " Cleft Lip ," and " Cleft Lip Repair ," for more detailed information.

retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, a complication can be associated with the presence of multiple open wounds that themselves are not complicated, indicating that a broader clinical complication may arise even when each individual wound does not exhibit additional problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2159
retrieved_docs.content: The category “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” includes the specific procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” as a child concept. This indicates that تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي, which involves the placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is classified among the non‑therapeutic interventions performed on the respiratory system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1736
retrieved_docs.content: Traction, splints, and other wound‑care modalities are understood to have a broader relationship with the term “الجر,” the Arabic word for traction, indicating that these interventions are encompassed within the larger concept of traction in wound management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
retrieved_docs.content: Abdomen repair and pelvis repair are classified under the Arabic procedural category رأب البوق, an umbrella term for female‑organ related interventions that links to the broader group “other or therapeutic procedures; female organs.” Rأب الردبة is a subtype of abdomen repair, and both repairs are hierarchically placed beneath رأب البوق. Reconstructive surgical procedures form the methodological foundation for many organ‑specific repairs, including the Williams‑Richardson operation for vaginal construction, cardiac valve repair (رأب الصمام القلبي), corneal repair (رأب القرنية), tympanic membrane repair (رأب طبلة الأذن), facial tightening (شد الوجه), skin grafting (طُعم الجلد), and treatment of atrial septal defect (علاج عيب الحاجز الأذيني). These procedures are grouped under “other or therapeutic procedures; female organs” and are subject to coding constraints that prohibit simultaneous billing with certain mutually exclusive interventions. The CTRP Terminology and its sub‑system CTRP

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 215
retrieved_docs.content: is mapped to the reconstructive surgical procedures category and is identified as a form of other aftercare, indicating that it functions as a follow‑up or supportive care measure after reconstructive interventions. Overall, the knowledge network connects abdominal and pelvic repairs to specific Arabic‑named female‑organ procedures, situates those procedures within a general therapeutic category, and integrates a wide range of reconstructive surgeries—including vaginal construction, ocular and ear membrane repairs, facial rejuvenation, and cardiac defect correction—under a unified framework of reconstructive surgical care and its associated aftercare.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2697
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx is linked to the procedure described by the Arabic term “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي,” indicating that laryngeal injection involves or leads to the placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway. Similarly, respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is classified under the same Arabic‑language category “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي,” showing that endotracheal intubation with assisted ventilation is considered a form of oral‑pharyngeal airway insertion. Together, these relationships illustrate that both laryngeal injection and respiratory intubation with mechanical ventilation are integral components of airway management techniques that fall within the broader concept of oral and pharyngeal airway placement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 5 -- Community 2928
retrieved_docs.content: The “Allowed qualifier” designation indicates that radiotherapy is recognized as an appropriate therapeutic option for certain medical conditions but not for others. Radiotherapy is an approved treatment modality for ورم الشفة (lip tumor), where it can be used alone or in combination with surgery to achieve local control of malignant disease. In the context of متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة (AIDS), radiotherapy is not a direct therapy for the immunodeficiency itself; however, it may be employed as an allowed intervention for opportunistic malignancies that can arise in patients with AIDS, such as Kaposi sarcoma or non‑Hodgkin lymphoma. Conversely, radiotherapy is not a standard or appropriate intervention for nervous system congenital anomalies; structural developmental defects of the nervous system are generally managed by surgical, rehabilitative, or supportive measures rather than by radiation. Recognizing these distinctions helps clinicians apply radiotherapy where it is evidence‑based—such as for lip tumors and certain cancers in AIDS—while avoiding its use in conditions like congenital nervous system anomalies where it offers no therapeutic benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2799
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as an etiological factor for several distinct medical conditions, including the bladder fistula known as “ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ,” the acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (“متلازمة نقص المناعة المكتسبة”), death (“التموت”), nervous system congenital anomalies, cerebral palsy (“فالج”), and cleft lip tumor (“ورم الشفة”). In addition, the same qualifier is associated with radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) as a therapeutic or relevant intervention for each of these conditions. Radiotherapy itself is further linked to nervous system congenital anomalies, death, cerebral palsy, and cleft lip tumor, underscoring its applicability or impact across this spectrum of diseases. This integrated view highlights that the allowed qualifier functions both as a recognized cause and as a treatment‑related concept for a range of serious health issues, emphasizing its importance in understanding disease mechanisms and in planning appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Combined obstetrical version is a category of obstetric procedures, and internal and combined version without extraction is a specific type within this category, indicating that the latter is a subclass of the former. This relationship clarifies that internal and combined version without extraction falls under the broader concept of combined obstetrical version.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198
retrieved_docs.content: Nasogastric tube procedures classify the nasogastric tube, indicating that the range of clinical actions, techniques, and protocols are organized around the use, insertion, management, and care of the nasogastric tube.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1772
retrieved_docs.content: Antenatal/postnatal care is linked to the concept of a “Mutilerende ingreep voor bevalling” (a mutilating procedure for delivery). The clinical entity Delivery finding is classified both as a Mutilerende ingreep voor bevalling and as the event described by “baby is coming out.” This dual classification indicates that findings related to delivery may be viewed as invasive or potentially harmful procedures while simultaneously representing the moment when the infant emerges, highlighting the relationship between routine antenatal/postnatal care and the possible need for or consequences of such delivery interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2833
retrieved_docs.content: The category “الإجراءات العلاجية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (Therapeutic procedures, not classified elsewhere) includes “جراحة” (Surgery) as a child concept, indicating that surgery is considered a specific type of therapeutic procedure within this broader, uncategorized therapeutic classification.
